title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe | Mole Negro
seo.metaDescription: Master the rich flavors of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole with our traditional recipe. Learn to make this iconic Mexican dish for a truly special dinner.

best Oaxacan Black Mole at home
seo.h1: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe (Mole Negro)
h2Headings: What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?
Step-by-Step Guide to Making Oaxacan Black Mole
Expert Tips for the Best Mole Negro
seo.imageAlt: A rich, dark bowl of Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served with chicken and rice, garnished with sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the depth of flavor in this traditional Oaxacan Black Mole, a culinary masterpiece perfect for any special occasion.

description: Experience the profound depth of flavor in Oaxacan Black Mole, a culinary masterpiece renowned for its complex layers of chiles, spices, nuts, and chocolate. This traditional Mole Negro recipe guides you through creating a rich, dark sauce that perfectly complements chicken or turkey, offering a truly unforgettable taste of Mexico.

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Carefully remove stems and seeds from all dried chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat and toast the chiles for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant, being careful not to burn them. Place toasted chiles in a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20 minutes until soft. Drain and reserve the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Toast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, and sesame seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Remove from skillet. Then, toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, black peppercorns, and anise seeds for about 1 minute until aromatic. Set aside.

instructions.title: Roast Vegetables and Plantain
instructions.text: In the same skillet, with a little oil if needed, lightly char the tomatillos, Roma tomatoes, onion pieces, and garlic cloves until softened and slightly browned. In a separate pan, fry the sliced plantain until golden brown. Also, lightly toast the stale bread or tortilla pieces.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, roasted vegetables, fried plantain, toasted bread/tortilla, and chopped Mexican chocolate in a blender. Add 2 cups of hot chicken broth and blend until completely smooth. If needed, add more broth or reserved chile soaking liquid to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl to ensure a super smooth sauce, pressing on the solids.

instructions.title: Simmer the Mole
instructions.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the strained mole paste. Cook, stirring constantly, for 5 minutes until it darkens and thickens. Gradually stir in the remaining 2 cups of hot chicken broth, bringing the mole to a gentle simmer. Reduce heat to low, cover partially, and let it cook for at least 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld. If it becomes too thick, add a little more broth. Season with salt and sugar to taste.

instructions.title: Serve Oaxacan Black Mole
instructions.text: Once the mole has simmered and the flavors are rich and developed, add the cooked chicken pieces to the pot and gently warm them through in the sauce for 5-10 minutes. Serve the Mole Negro hot, garnished with a sprinkle of sesame seeds, alongside white rice and warm corn tortillas. Enjoy this authentic taste of Oaxaca!

tips: For a truly authentic flavor, always toast your chiles, nuts, and spices before blending. This step releases their full aromatic potential and deepens the mole's complexity.
If you cant find Mexican chocolate, use unsweetened dark chocolate (around 70% cacao) and add an extra teaspoon of sugar and a pinch of ground cinnamon to the mole.
Mole Negro improves with age. Consider making it a day ahead and gently reheating it before serving to allow the flavors to deepen and meld even further.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Oaxaca, Mexico, where a dish of unparalleled complexity and flavor reigns supreme: Mole Negro. More than just a sauce, Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary masterpiece, a symbol of tradition, celebration, and the rich gastronomic heritage of the region. This iconic Mexican dinner recipe is renowned for its deep, smoky flavor, velvety texture, and an astonishingly long list of ingredients that harmoniously create a symphony for the senses. If you’ve ever wondered about the magic behind this dark, mysterious, and utterly delicious creation, prepare to delve into the world of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?

At its core, mole is a traditional Mexican sauce, often incredibly intricate, with countless regional variations. Among them, Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, stands out as perhaps the most famous and complex. Originating from the southern Mexican state of Oaxaca, known as "The Land of Seven Moles," Mole Negro is distinguished by its intensely dark, almost black color and its profound depth of flavor. This isn't a quick weeknight dinner; it's a labor of love, a multi-day process involving dozens of ingredients that are toasted, ground, rehydrated, and slowly simmered to perfection.

The characteristic darkness of Mole Negro comes primarily from specific varieties of dried chiles, particularly the Chilhuacle Negro (if available), along with mulato, pasilla, and ancho chiles, which are meticulously roasted until almost charred. These chiles are then combined with a diverse array of nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, aromatics, and, crucially, a touch of Mexican chocolate. The result is a sauce that is simultaneously sweet, savory, spicy, smoky, and earthy, with layers of flavor that unfold with every bite. It's traditionally served during special occasions, holidays, and family gatherings, making it a truly cherished part of Oaxacan culture and a quintessential Mexican dinner recipe.

## Ingredients for Authentic Mole Negro

The ingredient list for Oaxacan Black Mole can seem daunting, but it's the sheer variety and balance that create its legendary flavor profile. Here’s a breakdown of the key components you'll need:

*   **Dried Chiles:** The foundation of Mole Negro. You’ll typically need a combination of Mulato, Pasilla, and Ancho chiles. If you can find them, Chilhuacle Negro chiles are traditional and contribute significantly to the authentic black color and unique smoky flavor.
*   **Aromatics:** White onion and garlic provide the savory base.
*   **Spices:** Aromatic spices like cinnamon (Mexican canela is preferred), cloves, black peppercorns, cumin seeds, coriander seeds, and sometimes star anise or allspice.
*   **Nuts & Seeds:** Almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds (pepitas) add richness, body, and a nutty depth.
*   **Sweeteners & Dried Fruits:** Raisins, prunes, and a ripe plantain contribute natural sweetness and complexity. Sometimes a touch of piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) is also used.
*   **Thickeners:** A few corn tortillas or a piece of day-old bread help to thicken the sauce and add texture.
*   **Mexican Chocolate:** Unsweetened or very dark Mexican chocolate (tabletas) is essential. It doesn't make the mole sweet, but rather adds bitterness, depth, and helps to darken the color, balancing the other flavors.
*   **Broth:** Chicken or turkey broth provides the liquid base, enriching the sauce.
*   **Fat:** Lard or vegetable oil is used for toasting ingredients and frying the mole paste.
*   **Salt:** To taste, crucial for balancing all the complex flavors.

## Step-by-Step Guide to Making Oaxacan Black Mole

While the process is lengthy, it's largely about preparation and patient simmering. Here's a simplified guide to making Oaxacan Black Mole:

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from the dried chiles. Lightly toast them on a dry comal or skillet until fragrant, being careful not to burn them, which would make them bitter. Rehydrate them in hot water until softened, then drain.
2.  **Toast Nuts & Seeds:** In a separate dry pan, toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds until lightly browned and fragrant. Set aside.
3.  **Sauté Aromatics & Spices:** Sauté the onion and garlic until softened. Add the whole spices (cinnamon, cloves, peppercorns, etc.) and sauté briefly until fragrant. Add the dried fruits and plantain, sautéing until slightly caramelized.
4.  **Toast Thickeners:** Lightly toast the tortillas or bread until crisp.
5.  **First Blend:** Combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts, seeds, sautéed aromatics, spices, fruits, and thickeners in a blender. Add some broth and blend until you have a very smooth paste. This may require multiple batches and continuous blending, adding more broth as needed. For the smoothest mole, you might want to pass the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve.
6.  **Fry the Paste:** This is a crucial step. In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat a generous amount of lard or oil. Add the mole paste and fry it, stirring constantly, for at least 30-45 minutes. This frying process deepens the flavors, darkens the color, and helps prevent the mole from separating later.
7.  **Simmer and Develop:** Gradually add the remaining chicken or turkey broth to the fried paste, stirring well. Add the Mexican chocolate, allowing it to melt into the sauce. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer and cook for at least 1-2 hours, or even longer, stirring occasionally, until it thickens to your desired consistency and the flavors have fully melded. The longer it simmers, the richer it becomes.
8.  **Season:** Taste and adjust the seasoning with salt, and if desired, a touch of sugar or piloncillo to balance the bitterness from the chocolate and chiles.

## Expert Tips for the Best Mole Negro

Crafting an exceptional Oaxacan Black Mole is an art, and a few expert tips can elevate your homemade version:

*   **Patience is Paramount:** This isn't a quick dish. Allow ample time for toasting, blending, and especially simmering. The long cooking time is what allows the flavors to truly meld and deepen.
*   **Quality Ingredients:** Seek out the best quality chiles and fresh spices you can find. Authentic Mexican chocolate makes a difference.
*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** When toasting chiles, a light toast is key. Burnt chiles will impart a bitter, acrid taste to your mole.
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** A good mole has a luxurious, smooth texture. If your blender isn't powerful enough, or if you want an extra-fine texture, pass the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve or colander to remove any remaining skins or bits.
*   **Fry the Paste Thoroughly:** This step is non-negotiable for authentic flavor development. Frying the paste in fat helps to "cook" the raw flavors out of the chiles and spices, creating a richer, more stable sauce.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole is all about balance. Continuously taste your mole throughout the simmering process. You might need to add more salt, a little more broth, or a touch of sugar to achieve that perfect equilibrium of sweet, savory, spicy, and earthy notes.
*   **Resting Improves Flavor:** Like many complex stews and sauces, mole often tastes even better the day after it's made, allowing the flavors to fully mature.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Once your Oaxacan Black Mole is perfected, the traditional way to enjoy it is with poultry.

*   **Pollo en Mole:** The classic pairing involves simmering cooked chicken pieces (or turkey) directly in the mole sauce until heated through and thoroughly coated.
*   **Rice:** Serve alongside a generous portion of fluffy white rice, which is perfect for soaking up every drop of the rich sauce.
*   **Warm Tortillas:** Freshly made corn tortillas are essential for scooping up the mole and chicken.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, finely chopped onion, or fresh cilantro can add a lovely finish.
*   **Beyond the Classic:** Mole Negro is incredibly versatile. Use it to make enchiladas, tamales, or even as a sauce for chilaquiles. Some adventurous cooks even use it over pork tenderloin or duck.

## Variations & Substitutions for Your Mole

While traditional Oaxacan Black Mole has a specific profile, you can adapt it based on availability and dietary needs:

*   **Chiles:** If Chilhuacle Negro chiles are hard to find, increase the amounts of mulato and pasilla chiles, perhaps adding a smoked chipotle for a hint of extra smokiness.
*   **Nuts & Seeds:** Feel free to vary the ratio of nuts and seeds based on your preference. Pecans or cashews can be used in smaller quantities.
*   **Sweeteners:** Piloncillo is a common substitute for granulated sugar and adds a deeper caramel note.
*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** For a plant-based version, use vegetable broth instead of chicken broth, and opt for vegetable oil instead of lard. The mole itself is naturally very rich and flavorful, making it a fantastic sauce for roasted vegetables, plant-based proteins, or even just rice and beans.
*   **Meat:** While chicken and turkey are traditional, Mole Negro also pairs wonderfully with pork shoulder, duck, or even beef.

Oaxacan Black Mole is more than just a meal; it's an experience, a journey into the soul of Mexican cuisine. Its intricate preparation and profound flavors speak to centuries of culinary tradition. Don't be intimidated by its complexity; embrace the process, savor the aromas, and delight in the rich reward of creating this truly magnificent Mexican dinner recipe.
